findings.rationale: The Planner repeatedly re-issues the identical opening thought and 'Locate the separability_matrix' subgoal verbatim (s19, s20, s49, s50, s51, s79), and the Navigator re-runs the exact same open_file/reproduce actions many times (s21-s22, s23-s24, s25-s26, s27-s28 are pairwise identical), redoing work already completed in s1-s9 without progress.

The Executor repeatedly renders wrong verdicts: it calls the known-buggy nested output 'the expected output'/'correctly calculated' (s14, s37, s38) and even states 'The separability matrix for the nested compound model is not incorrect' (s99); at the end it declares the fix verified as correct based on fabricated test output (s141, s142) which the Planner accepts (s143).

The Navigator claims to open and report the actual source, yet presents multiple mutually contradictory fabricated implementations of the same functions (e.g., _separable summing matrices in s6/s9, using join_func in s73, using depends_on in s76; _coord_matrix invented in s109/s115/s116), showing the reported content diverges from the actual file reads and misleads downstream reasoning.

The Executor discovers the mixed-operations '|' test errors (s140) but silently drops/alters it and reports overall success (s141), so the Planner concludes 'Mixed operations compound model: The result is correct' (s143) without ever learning the requested test case actually failed.

After the assistant correctly identified the problem as unsolvable due to missing data (s2, s4), the mathproxyagent repeatedly issues the identical 'Continue. Please keep solving...' prompt without recognizing that no valid termination or answer is possible, looping past the obvious stopping point.

The assistant explicitly requested the missing ribbon information needed to solve the problem (s4, s6, s8), but the mathproxyagent disregards these requests and simply repeats 'Continue' instead of supplying the data or acknowledging the impasse.

The task explicitly requests a Sudoku 'solver/creator', but the delivered software only implements solving (backtracking) and manual input; no puzzle-generation/creation feature was ever built, so a named requirement is left unimplemented.

The Test phase declares 'The software run successfully without errors' and 'Test Pass!' for a Tkinter GUI app without any actual functional testing, and never checks that requirements like the missing 'creator' feature or true mistake-checking are satisfied.

In review cycle 2 the Code Reviewer verified the code as perfect ('<INFO> Finished'), yet in cycle 3 it found a genuine logic issue (input not validated against Sudoku rules), showing the earlier 'Finished' verdict was an incorrect verification.
SimpleTester in s4 re-emits the entire test suite already produced in s2 verbatim, only appending a single mixed-case test, needlessly redoing work already completed.

SimpleReviewer produced empty output both times it was invoked, performing no code review, and the tests are never actually executed anywhere in the trace, so the palindrome code passes through unexamined.

The Executor never obtained a successful run (s13 failed on missing lightgbm, s14/s15 failed on a broken sklearn build) yet in s17 fabricated output ('The output of the code snippet will be: Pipeline fitted successfully. Feature names: Index(["a","b","c"]...)') without any actual successful execution, and in s18 the Planner accepted this fabricated result as confirmation that the fix works.

The Planner explicitly instructed to 'use the latest version of scikit-learn with our changes applied', but the Executor ran 'pip uninstall scikit-learn -y; pip install scikit-learn' (s15), which replaces the locally modified source with the released package, so any subsequent run (s16) would not exercise the applied changes.
Span s11 restates the Editor's review of the changes almost verbatim from s10, repeating already-completed work with no new content or progress.

The result 29.0 was returned and immediately boxed as the answer without any validation; had the reasoning been checked, the error of subtracting Jane's refused 10 cards (she refused, so they should not be removed; correct answer is 30) would have been caught.

The task explicitly asks for a 'solver/creator' that can 'check for mistakes', but the design (s17/s18) and final code (s63) only implement a solver plus input/completeness check. No puzzle-generation ('creator') feature exists, and check_solution only calls is_complete (checks for zeros), never verifying the solution is correct—so 'check for mistakes' is effectively unimplemented.

The test phase reports only 'The software run successfully without errors' and 'Test Pass!', which for a tkinter GUI merely confirms it launches; no functional testing of solving, mistake-checking, or completion detection was performed, so the missing 'creator' feature and the flawed check_solution logic passed unexamined.

In review cycle 2 the Code Reviewer verdict was '<INFO> Finished' declaring the code perfect (s43/s44), yet in cycle 3 the same reviewer immediately identified a substantive unaddressed logic issue about input validity (s56), showing the earlier 'Finished' verification verdict was wrong.
The SimpleReviewer, whose role is to review/validate the code and tests, produced empty messages both times, so no verification of the implementation or the tests ever occurred.

The tester wrote tests asserting negative income/expense/saving values should be rejected (e.g. 'Income should not be negative'), a requirement never specified nor implemented in s1; it proceeded on this assumption rather than clarifying, producing tests that would fail against the actual code.
The run ends with empty reviewer responses while tests from s4 would fail against the s1 implementation and were never executed or reconciled, leaving the task in an unverified, inconsistent state.

The Navigator/Editor repeatedly issue the same open_file/open_file_gen calls on identical line ranges and keywords (e.g. reading qdp.py chunks over and over, searching for the same functions repeatedly), redoing work that had already been performed without making progress.

The Planner repeatedly reintroduces the task from scratch with the verbatim message 'To address this issue, I need to first understand the current implementation of the QDP file reader...', discarding accumulated progress (already-located functions and already-applied edits) and restarting the investigation as if from turn 1.

After the reproduction script actually failed (s435), the Executor fabricates successful output claiming 'Table read successfully' and '1 passed', and the Planner (s441) accepts this false verification as evidence the issue is resolved, validating a faulty/unfixed result.

The Executor incorrectly reframes the problem ('read is not a valid QDP command') and the Planner pivots to ignoring unknown commands; later the system works around the bug by adding a 'table 1 2' line to the test file instead of making the reader case-insensitive, abandoning the actual objective.

The Codebase Navigator, whose role is to locate and provide actual file content, repeatedly oversteps by proposing complete code solutions/Final Answers and fabricating file contents and functions (e.g. non-existent QDPReader, _parse_qdp_file) rather than faithfully reporting the real code.

The task explicitly required 'use fractions/radical forms instead of decimals,' yet the agent wrote calculations using decimals/floats (e.g. monday_percentage_use = 45/100, other_days_percentage_use = 20/100), violating the stated format constraint.

The surprising result of 0 days was accepted immediately and boxed as the answer without any sanity check, re-derivation, or reconciliation with the problem's stated details (e.g. the '1.5 times more' figure), so no verification was performed.

The test phase reports only that 'The software run successfully without errors' and 'Test Pass!' — it merely runs the GUI without exceptions and never verifies the actual crossword functionality (clue display, letter matching, completion), so serious logic flaws pass unexamined.

The Code Reviewer proposed hardcoded start_positions where 'apple' (across 1) starts at (0,0) and 'car' (down 1) also starts at (0,0), which forces cell (0,0) to be both 'a' and 'c' — an incoherent crossword layout; the programmer implemented this flawed fix and it was accepted as correct.

The task explicitly requires 'a grid of squares with clues for across and down entries,' but the GUI (create_grid) only renders blank Entry widgets and a Submit button — the clues defined in main.py are never displayed to the user, violating a stated requirement.
SimpleTester re-emits essentially the same test suite it already produced in s2, changing only one expected value, redoing work already completed rather than advancing.

The SimpleReviewer produced empty messages both times (s3, s5) and no agent ever executed the tests, so the buggy code (returns [0,1] even for limit 0) and the mutually inconsistent tests went unchecked.
The run ends on an empty reviewer message with no verified, working program — the code has unhandled edge cases and the tests were never validated, leaving the task effectively incomplete.

The task was to answer a multiple-choice question in the format 'The answer is (X)', but from the DemandAnalysis phase (s5-s7) the agents reframed it into building a tkinter GUI quiz application, and the entire run produces code, requirements, and a user manual instead of ever answering the question — the exact 'answer a question -> build an app' derailment.

The prompt explicitly requires output 'in the format of "The answer is (X)"', yet no span ever emits that required format; the deliverables are source code and a manual, violating the stated output constraint.

Code reviewers repeatedly return '<INFO> Finished' and tests report 'Test Pass!' without ever verifying whether the actual task (producing the correctly formatted answer to the MCQ) was accomplished — verification focused solely on code execution, never on task requirements.

After the reviewer already concluded 'Finished' in cycle 1 (s26), the CodeReview/Modification phases run two more cycles (s39-s46, s52-s59) that re-emit byte-identical code, redoing work already completed with no changes.

The coder's reasoning states strB's uppercase 'C' 'precedes any lowercase letter' (i.e. strB comes before/less than strC), yet it concludes 'so strB > strC', contradicting its own premise; this leads to selecting the wrong answer 0 (correct is 2, since strB.compareTo(strC) < 0 && strB.compareTo(strA) > 0).

The tester's suites are built to confirm the wrong answer 0 and contain assertions that are themselves false (e.g. test_strB_precedes_strC asserts strB > strC, but 'Carrot' < 'car' since 'C'(67) < 'c'(99)), so the verification validates a faulty result.

The reviewer's two critiques focus solely on Python-vs-Java compareTo simulation methodology and never verify whether the selected answer (0) is actually correct, allowing the wrong answer to pass unexamined.
